addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- bifrost-platform: Use this skill when an agent needs to deploy applications, manage infrastructure, authenticate, or troubleshoot delivery workflows through the Bifrost CLI. It teaches the agent to use the native `bifrost` binary directly, run a strong preflight for repo/app/platform context, prefer `git` before `gh`, pick sensible project/service defaults, and finish with deployment URLs and actionable diagnostics.
- implementation-planning: Produce an implementation plan so complete and unambiguous that a low- or medium-effort agent can build it mechanically, without re-deriving decisions. Use this whenever the user is planning, designing, scoping, or brainstorming a coding task and intends to implement it later — especially if they say things like "plan this", "design the approach", "before we code", "lay out the steps", or mention switching to a cheaper/faster model for the build. Trigger even when the user just describes a feature and asks how to approach it. The goal is a hand-off artifact: front-load all the reasoning here so implementation is translation, not invention.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Resolve git push permission denied error

content.text:
The error is clear: your SSH key is authenticating to GitHub as the account **`ajstudd`**, but you're pushing to **`junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension`** — and `ajstudd` doesn't have write access to that repo (or the repo doesn't exist yet).

Let me check the actual state.

content.text:
Confirmed: `ssh -T git@github.com` greets you as **`ajstudd`**, not `junaid-appointy`. GitHub picks the account by SSH key, and the key your SSH is offering belongs to `ajstudd`. Two things to sort out:

1. Does the repo `junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension` even exist?
2. How do you want to authenticate as `junaid-appointy`?

Let me check.

input.command: echo "---gh auth status---"; gh auth status 2>&1; echo "---repo exists?---"; gh repo view junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension 2>&1 | head -5; echo "---ssh config---"; cat ~/.ssh/config 2>/dev/null; echo "---loaded keys---"; ssh-add -l 2>&1

content.text:
Found it. You already have an SSH host alias set up for your junaid account, but the remote uses plain `github.com` which falls through to the `ajstudd` default key.

Look at your `~/.ssh/config`:
```
Host github.com-junaid
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_junaid
    IdentitiesOnly yes
```

The fix is to point the remote at the `github.com-junaid` alias instead of `github.com`. Let me verify that key exists and works first.

input.command: echo "---junaid key exists?---"; ls -l ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_junaid* 2>&1; echo "---auth as junaid alias---"; ssh -T git@github.com-junaid 2>&1

aiTitle: Resolve git push permission denied error

content.text: The `github.com-junaid` alias authenticates correctly as **`junaid-appointy`**. The fix is to repoint the remote to that alias.

input.command: git remote set-url origin git@github.com-junaid:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git && git remote -v && echo "---push---" && git push -u origin main 2>&1
input.description: Repoint remote to junaid alias and push

content.content:
origin	git@github.com-junaid:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git (fetch)
origin	git@github.com-junaid:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git (push)
---push---
To github.com-junaid:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git
 * [new branch]      main -> main
branch 'main' set up to track 'origin/main'.

origin	git@github.com-junaid:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git (fetch)
origin	git@github.com-junaid:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git (push)
---push---
To github.com-junaid:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git

content.text:
Done. Pushed successfully.

**What was wrong:** Your SSH was authenticating as `ajstudd` (the default key), but you were pushing to `junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension`. GitHub identifies the account by SSH key, so it correctly denied `ajstudd`.

**The fix:** You already had a host alias in `~/.ssh/config` for your junaid account (`github.com-junaid` → `id_ed25519_junaid`). I just repointed the remote to use it:

```
git@github.com-junaid:junaid-appointy/...   ✓ authenticates as junaid-appointy
git@github.com:junaid-appointy/...          ✗ falls through to ajstudd's key
```

**For future repos under this account**, remember to use the `github.com-junaid` host instead of plain `github.com` in the clone/remote URL. If you'd rather not remember, you could make junaid the default by adding a catch-all `Host github.com` block pointing at `id_ed25519_junaid` — but that flips which account is the "default," so only do that if junaid is the one you use most here.

content.text:
Plain `github.com` uses `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` (ajstudd). The junaid key is `~/.ssh/id_ed25519_junaid`. 

To make junaid the default for plain `github.com` URLs, I'll add an explicit `Host github.com` block pointing at the junaid key. I'll also preserve ajstudd under its own alias so that account doesn't become unreachable. Let me read the current config.

aiTitle: Resolve git push permission denied error

content.text: Now let me verify both identities resolve correctly and revert this repo's remote back to plain `github.com`.

input.command: echo "---plain github.com (should be junaid)---"; ssh -T git@github.com 2>&1; echo "---ajstudd alias (should be ajstudd)---"; ssh -T git@github.com-ajstudd 2>&1; echo "---revert this repo's remote to plain url---"; git remote set-url origin git@github.com:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git && git remote -v; echo "---confirm push path works (dry-run)---"; git push --dry-run origin main 2>&1
input.description: Verify both identities and revert remote to plain url

---plain github.com (should be junaid)---
Hi junaid-appointy! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.
---ajstudd alias (should be ajstudd)---

---revert this repo's remote to plain url---

content.text:
Done. The default identity is flipped, so plain `github.com` URLs now Just Work for junaid.

**New `~/.ssh/config` layout:**
| Host you write in a URL | Authenticates as | Key |
|---|---|---|
| `git@github.com:...` (default) | **junaid-appointy** | `id_ed25519_junaid` |
| `git@github.com-junaid:...` | junaid-appointy | `id_ed25519_junaid` |
| `git@github.com-ajstudd:...` | ajstudd | `id_ed25519` |

**Verified:**
- `ssh -T git@github.com` → `Hi junaid-appointy!` ✓
- `ssh -T git@github.com-ajstudd` → `Hi ajstudd!` ✓ (still reachable, just needs the alias now)
- This repo's remote is back to the clean `git@github.com:junaid-appointy/auxilio-extension.git` and pushes fine.

**One thing to note:** the roles are now reversed — `ajstudd` is the one that needs the `-ajstudd` alias. Any existing repo whose remote uses plain `github.com` but belongs to an `ajstudd`-only repo will now fail with the same "denied" error; repoint those to `git@github.com-ajstudd:...`. Given this machine's git user is `junaid-appointy` and your personal remotes are under that account, junaid-as-default is the right call.
